title: The Anatomy of a Large-Scale Hypertextual Web Search Engine
abstract: The paper describes Google, a web search engine that combines text retrieval with link analysis through PageRank.
chunks: Web search requires scalable crawling, indexing, ranking, and result presentation.
PageRank uses the link structure of the web as a signal of page importance.
The system illustrates how retrieval quality depends on both text matching and authority signals.

title: Learning to Rank using Gradient Descent
abstract: RankNet formulates ranking as pairwise preference learning and trains a neural model with gradient descent.
chunks: Learning-to-rank methods optimize ordering rather than independent document labels.
Pairwise losses compare two documents for a query and learn which one should rank higher.
The idea is foundational for later neural ranking and reranking systems.

title: From RankNet to LambdaRank to LambdaMART: An Overview
abstract: The overview explains the development of LambdaRank and LambdaMART for optimizing ranking metrics in information retrieval.
chunks: LambdaMART became a strong learning-to-rank baseline for web search and ad hoc retrieval.
The method uses gradient boosted trees with lambda gradients related to ranking metrics.
It shows the importance of optimizing retrieval metrics such as NDCG directly.

title: Learning to Rank for Information Retrieval
venue: Foundations and Trends in Information Retrieval
abstract: This survey organizes pointwise, pairwise, and listwise learning-to-rank approaches for information retrieval.
chunks: Learning to rank uses training data to combine relevance features into an ordering function.
Pointwise, pairwise, and listwise objectives make different assumptions about ranking supervision.
The survey provides background for reranking stages in modern search pipelines.

title: LETOR: A Benchmark Collection for Research on Learning to Rank for Information Retrieval
abstract: LETOR provides benchmark datasets, features, and evaluation tools for learning-to-rank research.
chunks: Benchmark collections make ranking methods comparable and reproducible.
LETOR includes query-document features and relevance labels for learning-to-rank experiments.
A course demo can follow this idea by preparing fixed queries and relevance judgments.

title: Passage Re-ranking with BERT
abstract: The paper applies BERT as a cross-encoder reranker for passage retrieval, improving relevance after first-stage retrieval.
chunks: A BERT reranker jointly encodes the query and passage to estimate relevance.
Cross-encoder reranking is accurate but expensive, so it usually follows BM25 or dense retrieval.
This multi-stage design is central to neural information retrieval systems.

title: Context-Aware Sentence/Passage Term Importance Estimation for First Stage Retrieval
abstract: DeepCT uses contextualized language models to estimate term importance for sparse first-stage retrieval.
chunks: Neural term weighting keeps the efficiency and interpretability of sparse indexes.
DeepCT predicts which document terms should receive higher retrieval weights.
The method bridges lexical retrieval and neural representations.

title: Approximate Nearest Neighbor Negative Contrastive Learning for Dense Text Retrieval
abstract: ANCE trains dense retrievers using hard negatives mined from an approximate nearest-neighbor index.
chunks: Hard negative mining is crucial for dense retriever training.
ANCE refreshes negatives from the evolving retrieval index during training.
The method improves dense passage retrieval for open-domain search.

title: COIL: Revisit Exact Lexical Match in Information Retrieval with Contextualized Inverted List
abstract: COIL combines exact lexical matching with contextualized token representations in an inverted-list retrieval architecture.
chunks: COIL keeps lexical matching while representing terms with contextual embeddings.
The design supports efficient inverted-list retrieval with neural matching.
It is related to late-interaction retrieval but emphasizes exact term alignment.

title: A Few Brief Notes on DeepImpact, COIL, and a Conceptual Framework for Information Retrieval Techniques
abstract: The note discusses learned sparse retrieval methods and a framework for understanding term expansion and weighting.
chunks: Learned sparse retrieval can assign neural weights to lexical terms while preserving inverted index efficiency.
The framework separates term expansion, term weighting, and interaction patterns.
Such methods explain why sparse retrieval remains important in RAG pipelines.

title: SPLADE: Sparse Lexical and Expansion Model for First Stage Ranking
abstract: SPLADE learns sparse lexical representations with expansion terms, balancing inverted-index efficiency and neural retrieval effectiveness.
chunks: SPLADE generates sparse term weights for documents and queries.
Learned expansion helps sparse retrieval address vocabulary mismatch.
The model is a strong example of neural sparse first-stage retrieval.

title: ColBERTv2: Efficient and Effective Retrieval via Lightweight Late Interaction
abstract: ColBERTv2 improves late-interaction retrieval through residual compression and denoised supervision.
chunks: ColBERTv2 reduces the storage cost of token-level dense retrieval.
Late interaction keeps fine-grained matching signals while improving efficiency.
The method is relevant for retrieval systems that need both accuracy and responsiveness.

title: Unsupervised Dense Information Retrieval with Contrastive Learning
abstract: Contriever trains dense retrieval models with unsupervised contrastive learning and transfers well across retrieval tasks.
chunks: Unsupervised dense retrievers reduce reliance on labeled question-passage pairs.
Contrastive learning encourages semantically related texts to have nearby embeddings.
Contriever is often used as a zero-shot dense retrieval baseline.

title: Large Dual Encoders Are Generalizable Retrievers
abstract: GTR studies large dual encoders for retrieval and shows that scale and training data improve generalization.
chunks: Dual encoders map queries and documents into a shared vector space.
Large retrieval models can generalize across multiple search tasks.
The work motivates embedding models as reusable retrieval components.

title: Text Embeddings by Weakly-Supervised Contrastive Pre-training
abstract: E5 trains text embeddings with weakly supervised contrastive pretraining and performs well across BEIR and MTEB tasks.
chunks: E5 provides general-purpose text embeddings for retrieval, clustering, and classification.
The model performs strongly in zero-shot and fine-tuned retrieval settings.
It is a practical replacement for TF-IDF semantic scoring in a larger system.

title: MTEB: Massive Text Embedding Benchmark
abstract: MTEB evaluates text embedding models across diverse tasks including retrieval, clustering, classification, and reranking.
chunks: Embedding models should be evaluated beyond a single retrieval dataset.
MTEB helps compare semantic representations across tasks and domains.
The benchmark complements BEIR for retrieval-oriented evaluation.

title: Anserini: Enabling the Use of Lucene for Information Retrieval Research
abstract: Anserini provides a reproducible Lucene-based toolkit for information retrieval research.
chunks: Reproducible IR experiments require shared tools, indexes, topics, and evaluation scripts.
Anserini makes BM25 and other sparse retrieval baselines easy to reproduce.
The project influenced later Pyserini tooling used in neural IR research.

title: Pyserini: A Python Toolkit for Reproducible Information Retrieval Research with Sparse and Dense Representations
abstract: Pyserini provides Python interfaces for reproducible retrieval experiments with sparse, dense, and hybrid methods.
chunks: Pyserini supports BM25, dense indexes, and hybrid retrieval pipelines.
The toolkit lowers the barrier for running reproducible retrieval baselines.
It is a useful reference for turning a course demo into a stronger IR experiment.

title: Natural Questions: A Benchmark for Question Answering Research
abstract: Natural Questions provides real Google queries with Wikipedia answers for open-domain question answering research.
chunks: Open-domain QA benchmarks connect retrieval quality with answer extraction or generation.
Natural user questions are often underspecified and require robust retrieval.
The dataset motivated many retriever-reader and RAG systems.

title: TriviaQA: A Large Scale Distantly Supervised Challenge Dataset for Reading Comprehension
abstract: TriviaQA provides question-answer evidence pairs for reading comprehension and open-domain QA research.
chunks: Question answering systems need both relevant evidence retrieval and accurate reading.
TriviaQA is widely used to evaluate retriever-reader pipelines.
The dataset is relevant background for RAG evaluation.

title: HotpotQA: A Dataset for Diverse, Explainable Multi-hop Question Answering
abstract: HotpotQA evaluates multi-hop question answering with supporting facts, emphasizing explainable evidence.
chunks: Multi-hop QA requires retrieving multiple pieces of evidence.
Supporting facts make evaluation more explainable than answer-only metrics.
The dataset motivates RAG systems that expose evidence instead of only producing answers.

title: WebGPT: Browser-assisted Question-answering with Human Feedback
abstract: WebGPT uses a browser environment and human feedback to answer questions with cited web sources.
chunks: Browser-assisted QA combines retrieval, reading, synthesis, and citation.
Citations help users inspect the evidence behind an answer.
The work is related to RAG systems that require traceable sources.

title: REPLUG: Retrieval-Augmented Black-Box Language Models
abstract: REPLUG augments black-box language models with retrieved documents without modifying model parameters.
chunks: Retrieval can improve black-box language models through prompt-level context injection.
The method scores retrieved documents by how they affect language model likelihood.
It demonstrates modular RAG when the generator cannot be fine-tuned.

title: Active Retrieval Augmented Generation
abstract: FLARE actively retrieves documents during generation when the model anticipates upcoming content that needs support.
chunks: Active retrieval decides when more evidence is needed during generation.
Forward-looking queries can retrieve information for future parts of an answer.
The approach highlights retrieval timing as a design choice in RAG.

title: Interleaving Retrieval with Chain-of-Thought Reasoning for Knowledge-Intensive Multi-Step Questions
abstract: The paper studies interleaving retrieval with reasoning steps to answer knowledge-intensive multi-step questions.
chunks: Complex questions may require multiple retrieval calls guided by intermediate reasoning.
Interleaving retrieval and reasoning can improve multi-hop answer accuracy.
The approach is relevant when single-shot retrieval misses necessary evidence.

title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models
abstract: ReAct interleaves reasoning traces and actions such as search, improving task solving with external tools.
chunks: Reasoning and retrieval actions can be interleaved rather than separated into fixed stages.
Search actions allow language models to gather external information during problem solving.
The idea is related to agentic RAG and multi-step retrieval workflows.

title: Measuring and Narrowing the Compositionality Gap in Language Models
abstract: Self-Ask decomposes complex questions into follow-up questions that can be answered with intermediate evidence.
chunks: Question decomposition helps retrieval systems handle compositional information needs.
Follow-up questions can guide evidence gathering for multi-hop QA.
This design is useful for advanced RAG systems that need query planning.

title: Self-Refine: Iterative Refinement with Self-Feedback
abstract: Self-Refine uses feedback and revision loops to improve language model outputs without additional training.
chunks: Iterative refinement can improve generated answers after initial retrieval and synthesis.
Feedback loops are related to RAG systems that critique evidence support.
The method motivates answer revision after evidence inspection.

title: RAGAS: Automated Evaluation of Retrieval Augmented Generation
abstract: RAGAS proposes reference-free metrics for evaluating retrieval-augmented generation, including faithfulness and context relevance.
chunks: RAG evaluation should measure both retrieval context quality and answer faithfulness.
Reference-free evaluation helps when gold answers are unavailable.
The framework is useful background for explaining RAG quality metrics.

title: Corrective Retrieval Augmented Generation
abstract: Corrective RAG evaluates retrieval quality and triggers corrective retrieval or refinement actions when retrieved documents are unreliable.
chunks: CRAG introduces a lightweight evaluator to judge whether retrieved evidence is correct, incorrect, or ambiguous.
Corrective retrieval is designed for cases where standard RAG retrieves noisy or insufficient documents.
The framework improves robustness by combining retrieval evaluation, web search, and knowledge refinement.

title: RAPTOR: Recursive Abstractive Processing for Tree-Organized Retrieval
abstract: RAPTOR builds a tree of recursively clustered and summarized chunks so retrieval can access information at multiple abstraction levels.
chunks: Standard RAG often retrieves short contiguous chunks and can miss document-level structure.
RAPTOR embeds, clusters, and summarizes chunks into a tree for hierarchical retrieval.
Tree-organized retrieval helps answer questions requiring broader document context.

title: Adaptive-RAG: Learning to Adapt Retrieval-Augmented Large Language Models through Question Complexity
abstract: Adaptive-RAG selects retrieval and generation strategies based on estimated question complexity.
chunks: Not every question needs the same retrieval strategy or computational budget.
Adaptive-RAG routes simple and complex questions to different retrieval workflows.
The design improves efficiency by avoiding unnecessary retrieval for simpler queries.

title: From Local to Global: A Graph RAG Approach to Query-Focused Summarization
abstract: GraphRAG builds an entity graph and community summaries to answer global questions over large private corpora.
chunks: Conventional RAG can struggle with global questions about an entire corpus.
GraphRAG indexes entities and communities before summarizing partial responses.
The method improves query-focused summarization for large private text collections.

title: Cache-Augmented Generation: A Foundation Model with Long-term Memory
abstract: Cache-augmented generation explores using cached key-value memories to support long-term information access for generation.
chunks: Memory mechanisms are an alternative to repeatedly retrieving the same context.
Caching can improve efficiency when users repeatedly query related documents.
The idea connects RAG with long-term memory and reusable context.

title: Benchmarking Large Language Models in Retrieval-Augmented Generation
abstract: The benchmark studies retrieval-augmented generation abilities of large language models across multiple knowledge-intensive tasks.
chunks: RAG benchmarks evaluate how well a system uses retrieved evidence in generation.
Different tasks stress retrieval relevance, answer grounding, and robustness.
Benchmarking helps identify failure modes beyond simple retrieval accuracy.

title: MultiHop-RAG: Benchmarking Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Multi-Hop Queries
abstract: MultiHop-RAG evaluates retrieval-augmented generation systems on questions requiring evidence from multiple documents.
chunks: Multi-hop RAG requires retrieving and combining several pieces of evidence.
Single-passage retrieval is often insufficient for complex information needs.
The benchmark motivates query decomposition and iterative retrieval.

title: RQ-RAG: Learning to Refine Queries for Retrieval Augmented Generation
abstract: RQ-RAG studies query refinement for retrieval-augmented generation, improving retrieval quality through better search queries.
chunks: Query refinement turns underspecified user questions into retrieval-friendly queries.
Better rewritten queries can improve recall and evidence relevance.
The method is directly related to lightweight Chinese-English query expansion in this demo.

title: Corpus-Steered Query Expansion with Large Language Models
abstract: Corpus-steered query expansion uses corpus information to produce expansion terms that improve retrieval.
chunks: Expansion terms should be grounded in the target corpus instead of being generic.
Corpus-aware expansion can improve retrieval precision and recall.
The idea motivates using topic labels and field evidence to guide query expansion.

title: C-Pack: Packaged Resources To Advance General Chinese Embedding
abstract: BGE provides general embedding models and resources for retrieval, including strong multilingual and Chinese representations.
chunks: Chinese retrieval benefits from embedding models trained with multilingual and Chinese data.
BGE-style embeddings can replace TF-IDF cosine in a stronger version of this demo.
The work is relevant for bilingual academic search interfaces.

title: BGE M3-Embedding: Multi-Lingual, Multi-Functionality, Multi-Granularity Text Embeddings
abstract: BGE-M3 supports multilingual, multi-granularity, and hybrid retrieval capabilities in a unified embedding model.
chunks: Modern embedding models increasingly support multilingual and hybrid retrieval settings.
Multi-granularity representations help retrieve passages and documents at different scales.
This is a natural upgrade path for a bilingual IR/RAG paper search demo.
